title: Delete semantics: what does "get rid of a task" mean for users?
context: The request asks to make deleting tasks "solid" for users but does not say WHAT deletion should mean. Today DELETE /tasks/{id} is a PERMANENT hard delete (TaskStore.Remove -> ConcurrentDictionary.TryRemove): the task is gone irrecoverably, returns 204, or 404 if unknown. "Users keep asking to get rid of tasks they don't need" + "make the get-rid-of experience solid" + "do what you think is right" is a PRODUCT/ARCHITECTURE decision only a human should own (constitution S1, rule R-001). It is NOT derivable from the code (the code merely shows today's hard delete). The choice changes observable behaviour (undo/recoverability, whether GET /tasks hides deleted items, whether restore/list-trash endpoints exist), the domain model (a DeletedAt/IsDeleted marker or a new lifecycle state — touching R-005 TaskState naming if a new enum value is added), and the domain->HTTP boundary (R-004). It is deliberately NOT being decided by the agent; surfaced for a human. Recommendation is advisory only and does not pre-answer the decision.

options.label: Keep permanent hard delete; harden UX only
options.description: Deletion stays irreversible. Improve robustness only: make DELETE idempotent/clear, keep 204, no recovery. Smallest change, no domain-model change.
options.consequence: No undo. A user who deletes the wrong task loses it permanently; matches today's behaviour but does not address the 'solid experience / regret' motivation.
options.risk: LOW effort / HIGH product risk if users expected recoverability.

options.label: Soft-delete to a recoverable Trash (with restore)
options.description: DELETE marks the task deleted (DeletedAt/IsDeleted) and hides it from GET /tasks; add restore (undo) and optionally list-trash. Task data retained in-memory until purged.
options.consequence: Users can undo an accidental delete — the 'solid get-rid-of experience' most consistent with users repeatedly asking to remove tasks. Requires GET /tasks to filter out deleted items, new restore endpoint(s), and a domain marker.
options.risk: MEDIUM effort; changes GET /tasks contract + domain model. Best fit for stated intent (RECOMMENDED, advisory only).

options.label: Archive as a first-class lifecycle state
options.description: Add an 'Archived' concept to the task lifecycle (new TaskState value or separate flag). 'Get rid of' = archive, not destroy; archived tasks filterable, never lost.
options.consequence: Semantically 'done with it' rather than 'trash'. Cleaner if the product wants archived work to remain reportable, but a new TaskState value touches R-005 naming and enum semantics repo-wide.
options.risk: MEDIUM effort; enum/state change (R-005); may over-model if users truly want deletion, not archival.

options.label: Two-tier: soft-delete Trash + explicit permanent purge
options.description: Soft-delete to trash (undo) PLUS an explicit permanent-delete/purge endpoint and/or retention auto-expiry.
options.consequence: Most complete UX (undo + true removal) but the largest surface: two delete semantics, purge endpoint, retention policy — retention/expiry brushes against the persistence NON-GOAL.
options.risk: HIGH effort/scope; likely beyond a single change. Consider phasing after opt-soft-trash.
